My field is interpersonal communication and the class that I'm gonna talk about is communication and conflict. I've taught it for about 16 years. And back at the beginning, what I would do is I would explain what research said about what a lot of the sticking points are in the kinds of interpersonal conflicts that are very hard to resolve. And then I would talk about what the research showed about communication behaviors you could use in the middle of a conflict conversation that improved the odds that you could resolve it collaboratively without it escalating or becoming destructive. We would talk through all that and the students get very excited and they'd say, I'm gonna walk right out the door and I'm gonna use this in my relationships. And they would treat their life as their laboratory 16 years later. That doesn't happen as much for a few reasons. People's attention spans have shortened. We have COVID survivors, we have a lot of people who just have so much social anxiety that the idea that they're gonna start a tricky conversation just stops them dead in their tracks. And what they really crave is at the moment, they learn about this, they wanna play test it, they want some kind of sandbox, some kind of opportunity to test drive what we're talking about. And so about maybe five years ago, I stumbled across a column that appears in The Guardian, the British newspaper called You Be the Judge. And this thing is wonderful. People write in with conflicts that they have and the columnist is really good about contacting both sides and getting details about their position. And when I found this, I thought, I'm gonna bring these to class and we're gonna role play them. And we tried it a few times, but the problem is when you have college students role-playing a conflict that's not real, that they're not really involved in what you get is a lot of nervous giggling. You get a lot of people reading what they have in a monotone and it just doesn't work very well as experiential learning. But the last time I taught the conflict class, I got the idea to take columns from you be the judge, and to give 'em the chat GPT, and turn on the voice controls and then have students role play the conflict with chat GPT. And it worked really, really well. I'm gonna demonstrate this really quickly. The column that I've put in is about a guy named Edward, who's married to a woman named Alice and she grew up in a family with about six siblings and they shared everything. So anytime he and his wife go and visit the family, they help themself to his toothbrush and he doesn't like that. And he wants help getting her siblings, his uh, sisters and brothers-in-law to leave his toothbrush alone. And I'm not making it up. By the way, that's January 12th, 2024. If you want to go look it up. At any rate, I put the column into chat GPT, and this is the prompt I gave it. I am a college student enrolled in a class about interpersonal conflict. The attached PDF is a case study of a conflict. I wanna practice my skills by roleplaying this case study. I will role play as Edward and you should role play as Alice. Please let me speak first. And Chad, GPT replied. Got it. I'll play Alice and stay true to her perspective from the case study. You can go ahead and start as Edward. Here we go. Alice, you know, I think that your family is great and you've explained to me before how they grew up, but I just can't help the fact that it creeps me out when they use my toothbrush. Alright, Edward, I do hear you. And look, I know it probably feels really frustrating honestly, in my family. We just never thought it was a big deal because we grew up that way. Do you think if we have that kind of direct chat, isn't there a danger that that means they're gonna kind of separate me out and not feel the way about me the way that they do about you? I totally get that concern, and I know that's a bit of a tricky balance. Honestly though, I think if we frame it as more of a comfort thing and less of a rejection of their closeness, they'll get it. Like we can reassure them that you do wanna be part of the family and that this is just one personal boundary. It doesn't mean you are not trying to fit in. So it doesn't come off as you pushing them away, just you wanting a little space on this one thing. I think they'll understand if we put it that way. So right away I saw improvement. But it means I can also talk to students about when you do a research project, if you go out having done the preparation to know what you're looking for, and if you curate a list of studies or other research material and you give that to Chad GPT and ask it to propose an outline to propose a framework which you then carefully check with the materials you brought to it, you get so much better results than if you just go to chat GPT and say, do my assignment for me.
